one_line_takeaway: MeGU uses a zero-shot MLLM to assign semantically-grounded 'perturbing labels' and a positive/negative feature-noise pair to disentangle shared vs. unique class features during unlearning fine-tuning, reporting closer retain-accuracy alignment to the gold retrained model and lower membership-inference-attack scores than UNSIR, Amnesiac, and SSD baselines across CIFAR-10/20/100 class-wise, sub-class, and random unlearning tasks.
key_finding: On CIFAR-20 superclass unlearning (ResNet18, class 'veg'), MeGU/MaGA reaches 82.53% retain accuracy versus the gold retrained model's 82.24% and FT's 72.56%, while driving forget-set accuracy to 0.00% (Table 2). On CIFAR-100 class-wise unlearning with ViT (class 'rocket'), the paper reports MeGU/MaGA narrows the retain-accuracy gap to the gold model by 0.35 percentage points relative to SSD. For MIA-based privacy leakage, the paper reports MeGU/MaGA achieves MIA scores 9.8% and 49% lower than SSD and UNSIR respectively on CIFAR-20 'veh2' with ViT (Table 2), and 86% and 69% lower than SSD and UNSIR respectively on the sub-class 'sea' with ViT (Table 3). In sub-class unlearning of 'rocket' with ViT, MeGU/MaGA improves retain accuracy by 2.51 and 3.71 percentage points over UNSIR and Amnesiac respectively (Table 3). In random unlearning on CIFAR-10, MIA scores are reported 40% and 79% lower than SSD on ResNet18 and ViT respectively (Table 4). For compute, MeGU/MaGA is reported to reduce unlearning time by over 59% compared to full retraining on CIFAR-100 class-wise unlearning with ViT (Figure 4). The paper's own stated limitation is that MLLM inference makes MeGU not the most time-efficient unlearning method among the baselines, though it argues this is mitigable via zero-shot-only MLLM use and precomputing/reusing the transition matrix.
core_contribution: The paper proposes MeGU (Machine-Guided Unlearning), a machine-unlearning framework for image classifiers with two novel components: (1) MLLM-guided perturbing-label assignment, where a zero-shot multi-modal LLM (MMICL) is prompted with binary True/False questions on a small exemplar subset to estimate inter-class feature similarity, which is cached as a lightweight transition matrix T and reused (without further MLLM calls) to assign each forget-set instance a semantically 'meaningful but incorrect' perturbing label via a top-tau ranking rule (tau=0.3); and (2) the 'Fragment-Align' strategy, which derives a positive feature noise (optimized to align with the perturbing label) and a negative feature noise (optimized adversarially against the original label) from the frozen pretrained model, combines them as a weighted sum (alpha=0.7) added to forget-set inputs, and fine-tunes the model on this perturbed forget set plus a 10,000-instance retain subsample. The motivating analysis (that semantic concepts share 'associated' feature patterns while retaining class-unique features, and that existing unlearning methods fail because they don't disentangle the two) is presented as the paper's own conceptual contribution, not adopted from cited work.
threat_model: Not an adversarial-attack paper in the classic sense; the paper's own security framing is privacy-motivated: unlearning is positioned as a response to 'Right to be Forgotten' / GDPR-style deletion requests, and the paper explicitly evaluates whether an unlearned model still leaks memorized information about the forgotten data using a Membership Inference Attack (MIA) score (Shokri et al. 2017) as one of its three core metrics, stating 'lower MIA probabilities indicate better security in adversary attacks.' The implicit adversary is one who queries the unlearned model to infer whether specific forget-set samples were part of training; accuracy-based verification alone is treated as insufficient (the paper cites the 'Streisand effect' as a reason gold-model-alignment on accuracy, not raw forgetting, is the target). No poisoning, extraction, or jailbreak threat model is present or implied.
limitations: Author-stated limitation: reliance on MLLM inference (for transition-matrix estimation) imposes a computational cost that makes MeGU less time-efficient than lighter baselines, though the authors argue this can be amortized by precomputing the transition matrix once and reusing it, or reducing the number of exemplars used. Additional limitations evident from the text but not explicitly flagged by the authors: evaluation is restricted to image classification on three CIFAR-derived datasets with two backbones (ResNet18, ViT) -- no evaluation on other modalities, larger-scale datasets, or LLM-scale unlearning is presented in the sections extracted; hyperparameters tau=0.3 and alpha=0.7 are described as tuned empirically via sensitivity studies (referenced as Appendix E, not present in the extracted pages) rather than derived analytically; in the random-unlearning setting (Table 4) the paper itself notes that 'unlearning performance cannot be fully measured by accuracy alone' because retain and forget sets share classes, undercutting the strength of the accuracy-alignment claims specifically for that scenario; and the paper's own tables show inconsistent method naming (MeGU vs. MaGA) which is a presentation/proofreading defect in the manuscript itself.

quantitative_results: CIFAR-20 'veg' class-wise unlearning, ResNet18: MeGU/MaGA retain accuracy 82.53% vs. retrain (gold model) 82.24% vs. FT 72.56% (Table 2)
CIFAR-100 'rocket' class-wise unlearning, ViT: MeGU/MaGA narrows retain-accuracy gap to gold model by 0.35 percentage points versus SSD (Section 5.2 text)
CIFAR-20 'veh2' class-wise unlearning, ViT: MeGU/MaGA MIA score 9.8% lower than SSD and 49% lower than UNSIR (Section 5.2 text, Table 2)
CIFAR-20 sub-class 'sea', ViT: MeGU/MaGA MIA score 86% lower than SSD and 69% lower than UNSIR (Section 5.2 text, Table 3)
CIFAR-20 sub-class 'rocket', ViT: MeGU/MaGA retain accuracy improves 2.51 points over UNSIR and 3.71 points over Amnesiac (Section 5.2 text, Table 3)
CIFAR-10 random unlearning: MeGU/MaGA MIA score 40% lower than SSD on ResNet18 and 79% lower than SSD on ViT (Section 5.2 text, Table 4)
CIFAR-100 class-wise unlearning, ViT: MeGU/MaGA reduces unlearning compute time by over 59% versus full retraining (Figure 4, Section 5.2 text)
baselines_compared: retrain (gold model, trained from scratch without forget data)
Bad Teacher (Chundawat et al. 2023, labeled 'teacher', random-unlearning table only)
